questions.optionB: Painful red eye with ciliary flush, photophobia, miosis, cells and flare in anterior chamber, and keratic precipitates on corneal endothelium
questions.explanation: Core concept: Anterior uveitis = iritis/iridocyclitis → painful, photophobic, miosis, cells+flare in AC. Why correct: Ciliary flush (circumcorneal congestion), photophobia, miosis (spasm of sphincter pupillae), cells (WBCs in AC), flare (protein leakage), KPs (inflammatory deposits on corneal endothelium). Mutton-fat KPs in granulomatous uveitis (sarcoidosis, TB). Why A wrong: Painless red eye → consider episcleritis, subconjunctival hemorrhage. Why C wrong: Proptosis → orbital tumors, thyroid eye disease, orbital cellulitis. Why D wrong: Halos → corneal edema (acute angle closure glaucoma). Clinical relevance: Associations: HLA-B27 (ankylosing spondylitis, Reiter's), sarcoidosis, TB, JIA (children — chronic). Treatment: topical steroids + cycloplegics (atropine — to prevent posterior synechiae). Exam trap: Uveitis pupil = miotic (small, irregular due to synechiae); ACG pupil = mid-dilated, fixed. Distinguish! Memory aid: 'Uveitis = miUte pupil (small) + cells flare + pain.' Reference: Khurana – Uveal Tract.
questions.conceptTag: Anterior uveitis ciliary flush miosis cells flare KPs
